title: Minutes point to another RBA rate rise in August
full_text:
The Reserve Bank of Australia is tilting toward an interest rate hike in August, with the board stating bluntly in the minutes of its June meeting that it will do what it “considers necessary” to tame inflation – including “increasing the cash rate target if necessary”.
The warning was issued during deliberations ahead of the board’s decision to hold the benchmark rate at 4.35 per cent earlier this month – and makes clear that homeowners are not yet out of the woods on further increases to their monthly mortgage payments.
“In the minutes, the hawkish bias came through loud and clear,” IG markets analyst Tony Sycamore said.
This year, the RBA has hiked rates three times in a bid to strangle inflation, which is running at a headline annual rate of 4 per cent, well above the target band of 2-3 per cent.
Alongside the warning, the minutes also reveal that the board expects another spike in inflation in the June quarter.
“Members noted that information received since the previous meeting had supported the view that the economy was operating with excess demand and widespread inflationary pressures,” the minutes state.
“Inflation was still materially above the board’s target and the staff’s expectation remained for underlying inflation to increase in the June quarter.” The June quarter CPI numbers come in on July 29, just days before the RBA’s meeting on rates across August 10-11.
The board also expects inflation to be above target for a further two years. Last week, the ABS reported a dip in headline inflation in the year through to May from 4.2 per cent to 4 per cent.
But the trimmed mean measure, which strips out volatile items, was 3.6 per cent, up from 3.4 per cent.
A fourth hike would have taken the cash rate to its highest level since 2011 and added an extra $114 on monthly payments on an average loan size of $736,000, Compare the Market said.
Reserve Bank Governor Michele Bullock has also stated consistently that she is willing to slow the economy through rate hikes to curtail inflation.
“We are forecasting that growth is going to slow and growth has got to slow … that is what is needed to try and get inflation down,” she said after the RBA’s June decision to hold rates. There is no language in the minutes that suggest the board is considering a cut in the near term, though it does say financial conditions are now “probably somewhat restrictive”.
